notes: Official Origin product capture for TreasuryBench V1.
response:
Your accounts show a HealthEquity HSA already connected, which is a great foundation. Whether to prioritize HSA contributions this year depends on a few key factors — let me walk you through the 2026 limits, the strategic case for HSAs, and the enrollment caveats that could trip you up.
The strategic case for prioritizing your HSA
The HSA is genuinely the most tax-advantaged account available in the US tax code — it's the only account that offers a triple tax benefit:
Contributions reduce your taxable income dollar-for-dollar (pre-tax through payroll or tax-deductible if made directly)
Growth inside the account is completely tax-free
Withdrawals for qualified medical expenses are tax-free
After age 65, you can also withdraw for any reason and simply pay ordinary income tax — effectively making it a bonus traditional IRA at that point.
2026 HSA contribution limits
The IRS limits for 2026 are:
Self-only HDHP coverage: $4,400 per year
Family HDHP coverage: $8,750 per year
Catch-up (age 55+): an additional $1,000 per eligible spouse, contributed to that individual's own HSA
One important nuance if you're married: if either spouse has family HDHP coverage, both spouses are treated as having family coverage for HSA purposes. That means the combined contributions across both HSAs cannot exceed $8,750 (plus any catch-up amounts). You cannot stack a self-only limit on top of the family limit.
Enrollment caveats that matter
HDHP requirement: You must be covered by a qualifying High Deductible Health Plan to contribute at all. For 2026, that means a plan with at least a $1,700 minimum deductible (self-only) or $3,400 (family), and a maximum out-of-pocket no higher than $8,500 (self-only) or $17,000 (family).
Disqualifying coverage: You cannot contribute if you have a general-purpose FSA or HRA that reimburses medical expenses before your deductible, if you're enrolled in Medicare, or if you're claimed as a dependent on someone else's return. A limited-purpose FSA (dental/vision only) is typically fine.
Mid-year enrollment and proration: If you enrolled in your HDHP mid-year, your contribution limit is generally prorated by the number of months you were eligible as of the 1st of each month. For example, if you became eligible July 1, 2026, your self-only limit would be roughly $4,400 × 6/12 = $2,200.
The last-month rule: Alternatively, if you're HSA-eligible on December 1, 2026, you can contribute the full annual limit regardless of when you enrolled. The catch: you must remain HSA-eligible through all of 2027 (the "testing period"). If you lose eligibility during that window — say you switch to a non-HDHP plan or enroll in Medicare — the excess contribution above your prorated amount becomes taxable income plus a 10% penalty.
Recommended prioritization framework
Since I don't have your income, tax filing status, or whether your HDHP is self-only or family coverage in your profile yet, here's how I'd think about the ordering generally:
First: Capture any employer 401(k) match — that's an immediate 100% return
Second: Max your HSA — the triple tax advantage is hard to beat, especially if you can invest the balance and pay medical expenses out of pocket now to let the account compound
Third: Max your 401(k) to the $24,500 limit
Fourth: IRA contributions ($7,500 for 2026)
Since tax and retirement account decisions involve specialized expertise, I'll note that per CFP Board standards, consulting a qualified tax advisor or CFP for advice specific to your full financial picture before finalizing your contribution strategy is always worthwhile.
Would you like to walk through how your current HealthEquity HSA balance and employer contributions fit into the 2026 limit, or explore how to invest the HSA balance for long-term growth?
Note: Calculations, potential savings, and eligibility determinations are estimations for illustrative purposes only. Tax laws change frequently. Consult a qualified tax professional before making decisions.
